entry.title: SAP BTP: Cloud Application Programming Model
entry.content:
SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Model exposes the ability to extend standard Cloud, on-premises and hybrid application functionality with flexible custom business logic scripted using the developer’s choice of programing language (SQL, SQL Script, Python, Node.js and JavaScript) and exposing the functionality to various user interfaces (UIs) e.g. Microsoft Power Bi, SAP Fiori, Dataiku and Google Big Query using various integration frameworks e.g. OData v4,JDBC/ODBC depending on the case study.

The developer takes pride in delivering a scalable extended functionality, embedding robust real-time monitoring capabilities to ensure a health life cycle and protecting the functionality with secure authentication protocols e.g. OAuth 2.0, JWT Bearer, SAML version 2.0 and RFC Specification.

Furthermore, the developer ensures a reliable and robust solutions by implementing continuous integration and continuous delivery CI/CD pipelines that automates testing, building and deployment of code changes this strategy ensures a speedy development and delivery cycle.

Traditionally developers extended business logic directly on-premises on the ABAP application server. With the ever-changing world of technology, digital transformation has become essential for organization to say afloat of competitors and therefore the programming model paradigms and capabilities of extending SAP solutions have evolved drastically and provides a seamless experience into the cloud.

Figure 1:

Architectural Paradigm Shift from Classic ABAP App server to CAP App server

Source: Thomas Jung, SAP HANA Extended Application Services blog, URL: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-hana-extended-application-services/ba-p/12963426/page/4

The SAP Cloud Application model comes with a rich set of languages, libraries, and tools for building enterprise-grade services and applications. It guides developers along a 'golden path' of proven best practices  and a great wealth of out-of-the-box solutions  to recurring tasks:

Figure 2:

SAP Cloud Application Model Architecture

Source HTTPS://Cap.cloud.sap , URL: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/about/

The below diagram categories the skill set of SAP Cloud Developer based on the scope of the development either being ABAP or Non-ABAP. Each with its unique set of tools and methodologies:

Figure 3:

 SAP Cloud Developer skill set

Source: Building side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP, URL: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/build-side-by-side-extensions-on-sap-btp/identifying-the-need-for-side-by-side-extensibility_f1e838f0-f02a-43b4-8896-cedc25a7d5d0

The Cloud Application Programming model enables a collaboration of agile cross functional teams inclusive of but not limited, depending on the scope of the development:  citizen developers (low-code/no-code), Professional developers (Scripting, OOP), business analysts, data engineers and application specialists to model robust data models:

Figure 4:

Agile cross functional team

Source: Investigating the Impact of Cross-Functional Teams on Business Success

URL: https://www.iienstitu.com/en/blog/cross-functional-teams

SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Model enables a culture where people regardless of their title or background, work together to imagine, develop, deploy, and operate a solution. This strengthens the trust between employees and authentic healthy work relationships.

Thank you all for taking a glimpse into the above Content. Please don’t for get to like, comment and share.

Abbreviations:

APP: Application

CAP: Cloud Application Programing

CDS: Core Data Services

CI/CD: Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery

JWT: JSON Web token

OData: Open Data Protocol

RFC: Remote Function Call

SAML: Security Assertion Markup Language

SQL: Structured Query Language

UIs: User Interfaces

entry.title: Architecting S/4HANA Extensibility: Full Stack Extensions with Clean Core Paradigm
entry.content:
Introduction

In the ever evolving landscape of enterprise applications, designing and developing a scalable and secure business applications is a crucial skill for developers, consultants and architects. This blog showcases a full stack project that brings together SAP and open source technologies to deliver a robust solution.

I have tried to showcase the important components of the project and how they are translated to executing blocks once deployed on cloud. The blog also touches cloud (Business Technology Platform) services which are important in many customer use cases. 

The intent of this blog is to provide you the starting point and give you a leverage to explore possibilities on your own and ofcourse, with the help of your preferred co-pilot!

At the end of the blog, I have also provided my GitHub repo where you can find a complete project for your reference.

I will breakdown the article into 3 parts

  1. Use Case
  2. Solution architecture
  3. Components of the architecture

Use Case

I have developed a full stack application adhering to the architecture and developed based on a use case. It is available on GitHub.

The README file takes you through each and every step starting from use case till full fledge enterprise solution.

The project is more than just a standalone application. It serves as a blueprint for SAP extensibility while adhering to the Clean Core strategy. By leveraging SAP BTP services, CAP, Fiori, and seamless S/4HANA integration, this approach ensures that businesses can extend their ERP capabilities without modifications to the core system.

This architecture is highly adaptable and can be referred to your specific use case.

Solution Architecture

Below diagram provides the architecture of the solution. It also shows the transition from design time to run time artifacts and how the components fit along with seamless integration with the BTP services.

Figure-1

Components of the architecture

In this section, let us go through all the components shown in the above diagram and understand the details around each one of it and how these components are translated to the code for the specific use case.

The complete solution is built on the robust SAP Business Technology Platform.

Development/Design Time

I have extracted a part of the architecture diagram below to focus on the design/development aspects:

Figure-2

Developer develops the application on Business Application Studio. When creating a full-stack CAP application, there are 6 important components which are explained below to showcase some critical files inside each of the directories.

  1. app - This directory contains all the UI content. This means, when you create UI apps (standard or freestyle FIORI), all the UI content for each app goes inside this folder. This is the face of your application.
    1. Take a look at the content of one of the UI apps - sales. You will see webapp folder which contains the UI code adhering to the MVC pattern. For standard FIORI you won't see much UI code in webapp as the UI is rendered using FIORI AppComponent using the OData annotations. Take a look at the freestyle FIORI app - commissionconfig. Here you will see controllers, views and stylesheets.
    2. The second important component inside the UI app is the xs-app.json. This is important as this is used by the application router to define routes/destinations and provide authentication type.
  2. db: This is the foundation of your application/project. Here you define the entity model and the relationships between the entities. Take a look at the schema.cds. This folder also holds the initial data to be loaded inside the data folder. 
  3. srv: This is the heart of your application. This directory holds the services and event handler where you can add annotations, custom logic and secure your entities. This folder also holds external API service entities. 
  4. xs-security.json: Securing your application is as important as developing it. XSUAA service in BTP is leveraged for this. It gives role based access to the resources of your application. Take a look at the file: xs-security.json. Here you will see role templates and scopes defined which are translated to your XSUAA instance at runtime.
  5. package.json: This is one of the important file which packages your project by defining the dependencies, start up scripts and most important cds configurations for development and production environments. Browse through this file: package.json.
  6. mta.yaml: This is the deployment descriptor and orchestrator. In this file you define the modules of your application, resources required by your application and dependencies. This file is used during the deployment of the project to cloud foundry environment. During deployment, as per the configuration provided in this file, service instances will be created and application will be deployed to the environment accordingly. Do not get overwhelmed by the structure of the file, when you start building the application, you will understand. Take a quick look at the file: mta.yaml. Refer the mta build tool help docs here - https://sap.github.io/cloud-mta-build-tool/

Deployment/Run Time:

Now, let us see how the above components translate after deployment. I have extracted a part of the architecture diagram below to focus on the runtime/deployment aspects:

Figure-3

When you deploy the project, using the multi target application descriptor file, the MTA deployer creates the resources defined in the resources section of the mta.yaml. Let's take an example of the HDI container and XSUAA service instance required by the application runtime i.e. srv. In the below fragment, you will see the module sales-commission-srv and dependencies in the requires section

If you go down in the resources section, you will see the dependencies for HDI container and XSUAA service.

  • If you observe the XSUAA resource, you will see it takes the xs-security.json during deployment and creates the scopes at the deployment time.
  • Once the HDI container is created, the entities described in schema.cds are created in the database container.

Now, let's come to the UI part and see how the artifacts are translated from design time to runtime during deployment

As you know, HTML5 repository provides a central place to store the static content of the HTML5 applications. Deep dive on HTML5 repository can be found here.

So, to add the UI content to the HTML5 repository on BTP, you have to add the service in your mta.yaml as shown in the below fragment

 Once you update the HTML5 repository, it will pull the UI content from your project.

Destination Service: The destination service is required to store the API destinations which can be used at runtime to connect to the backend APIs. In our project, we have a use case to connect to the backend API_BUSINESS_PARTNER. In package.json you will see the API_BUSINESS_PARTNER configuration.

You create the destination - BP_DESTINATION in the destination service which will be used by the application when connecting to the backend API.

The Application Router & SAP Build Work Zone: The last two critical pieces in the puzzle. These are the gateway to your application.

  • Application Router: The application router is the single point of entry for your application. This is the secured gateway to your application resources. You can leverage SAP managed application router or even extend the application router by installing it manually. At runtime, the application router fetches the xs-app.json from the UI content and uses it for routing and authentication leveraging XSUAA service. 
  • SAP Build Work Zone: This is the launch pad where you can launch your applications, group your applications and also hide/show applications based on roles. The managed application router is provided with SAP Build Work Zone subscription.

Conclusion

Drawing from my experience, I must emphasize that these insights can serve as a starting point, though their applicability may vary based on specific scenarios. Also there will be most probably customizations required as per your specific use case.

In summary, SAP BTP provides robust ecosystem that empowers enterprises with flexible integration, secure connectivity and efficient development. 

This project will surely give you a holistic view from design and development of cloud native application and eventually deployment on cloud in production scenarios.

It's never too late to start  —  GitHub Repo

Stay curious! Keep learning!

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/get-started/
  2. https://developers.sap.com/
  3. https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/#/topic/851bde42e4e1410c96abbe402fa9128c 
  4. https://api.sap.com/api/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER
  5. https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition/what-is-sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition
  6. https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/application-router

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Forced Upgrades & Scheduled Maintenance Windows
entry.content:
Maintenance Windows

 SAP HANA Cloud undergoes scheduled maintenance to ensure optimal performance and security. Typically, maintenance windows are planned on a weekly basis, but these are only utilized in exceptional circumstances to minimize disruptions. Major activities, such as significant software upgrades or system enhancements, are reserved for designated major upgrade windows. During these periods, SAP HANA Cloud engineers perform essential tasks like patch installations, security updates, and performance tuning. It's noteworthy that not all maintenance activities necessarily result in downtime. This proactive approach ensures that the cloud infrastructure remains robust, secure, and up-to-date, providing a reliable environment for users. Find out more information here.

Forceful Upgrade of SAP HANA Cloud Instance

A forced upgrade is a process where SAP automatically upgrades a HANA database version to a more recent release. 

Expected Behaviour: 

During a forced upgrade, an in-place upgrade is executed, leading to a restart of the affected instance. As the system re-establishes its in-memory structures, a performance ramp-up period is expected. This process does not facilitate an off-place upgrade or utilize a shadow process, commonly referred to as a near-zero downtime upgrade, despite these modes being configured in SAP HANA Cloud Central. Consequently, this enforced upgrade approach results in complete downtime, contrasting with more seamless upgrade methods.

Primary Objectives:
  • Security: Enhance protection against vulnerabilities.
  • Functionality: Introduce new features.
  • Efficiency: Improve performance and workflow.
Scheduling and Notification:
  • Maintenance Window: Automatic upgrades occur within a predefined maintenance window.
  • Notification: Customers are informed in advance about the maintenance window.
  • Tool: Use the Cloud System Notification Subscriptions tool (CSNS) to configure notifications regarding forced upgrades as well as planned and unplanned downtimes
  • Use of MUW: SAP typically schedules forced upgrades during the regular major upgrade window (MUW).
Exceptional Cases:
  • Severe Incidents: SAP may deviate from the MUW schedule for immediate action in case of critical issues affecting customers.
Out-of-Maintenance Versions:
  • Upgrade to New QRC: SAP will perform a forced upgrade for instances running on out-of-maintenance Quick Release Cycle (QRC) versions during a MUW.
  • MUW Selection: The specific MUW will be chosen based on the overall maintenance load.
  • No Guarantee: An immediate upgrade in the next MUW after the end of maintenance is not guaranteed.
Customer Responsibility:
  • Proactive Upgrades: Customers are advised not to rely solely on forced upgrades and should proactively upgrade their systems in advance. Upgrading in a regular cycle provides several benefits:
    1. You can choose the date of the upgrade.
    2. You can choose to upgrade without the need to restart the instance (you can avoid downtime using the option of a near-zero downtime upgrade).
    3. You can test new features and changes introduced in the new QRC version in advance.
    4. You can keep your instances up-to-date with the latest security patches and bug fixes.

Update Releases and System Support 

Release Cycle:
- New version of SAP HANA Cloud released every three months.
- Releases occur at the end of every quarter.

Version Identification:
- Quarterly Release Cycle (QRC) reference used for identification.
- Format: QRC <Quarter>/<Year> (e.g., QRC 1/2024).
- Technical version number format: <Year>.<Calendar Week>.<Patch Number> (e.g., 2024.2.3).

Maintenance Period:
- Each QRC update is maintained for seven months.
- Software patches for bug fixes and improvements are available during this period.

Patch Installation:
- Patches can be installed by administrators at their convenience.
- Patches are accessible from within SAP HANA Cloud Central.

Support After Maintenance Period:
- After seven months, a QRC is still supported but no longer maintained with patches.
- To use an actively maintained version, customers must update to a newer QRC version.
- SAP will continue to provide the support defined in your contract for all available QRC versions, including those that are out of maintenance.

I hope that the information provided will serve as a valuable guide for the seamless operation of your SAP HANA Cloud instance, ensuring a smooth and uninterrupted experience.

entry.title: SAP Data & Analytics Strategy: Current insights and future prospects
entry.content:
The SAP Business Technology Platform is an innovation platform that has been specially optimized for SAP applications in the cloud. It enables companies to extend and personalize SAP applications and to integrate and network entire IT landscapes. By bringing together data and analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), application development, automation and integration in a unified environment, SAP BTP helps companies to connect processes, make informed decisions and continuously drive innovation.

SAP BTP

A central area is data and analytics, which supports companies in strategically utilizing their data and making data-based decisions.

Data and analytics in SAP BTP focuses on:

  1. Data management: The platform provides functions for integrating, processing and organizing data from different sources. This enables a central database that is reliable and consistent.
  2. Analysis functions: With powerful analysis tools, companies can evaluate data in real time. This allows trends to be identified, predictions to be made and decision-making processes to be optimized.
  3. Integration of SAP and third-party data: SAP BTP combines data from SAP applications such as ERP and CRM with data from other sources. This promotes a holistic view of business processes.
  4. Scalability and security: The platform offers flexible and scalable data solutions that are tailored to the requirements of each company. It also ensures the highest security standards to protect sensitive data.

This area enables companies to use their data as a strategic advantage by creating a basis for innovation, increased efficiency and competitive advantages.

Integrated Data & Analytics Portfolio

With SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud, SAP offers three powerful solutions for data analysis, management and storage.

SAP Analytics Cloud is a comprehensive solution for analyses and planning scenarios. It enables companies to exploit the full potential of their data sources and maximize investments in business-critical applications. By integrating generative AI, reports can be automated, hidden insights gained and business plans developed. It also supports the consolidation of financial, supply chain and operational planning into one centralized solution.

SAP Datasphere gives data professionals seamless and scalable access to business-critical enterprise data. It provides reliable data by automatically reusing semantic definitions and associations from SAP applications. It also harmonizes heterogeneous data in a semantic business model and provides comprehensive access to all data in hybrid and cloud environments, regardless of where it is stored.

SAP HANA Cloud is SAP's database-as-a-service solution that combines powerful in-memory database technology with flexible data management capabilities. It enables companies to process, analyze and store large amounts of data in real time, supporting fast and informed decision-making. It also offers scalability and integration with other SAP and third-party applications to ensure a seamless data landscape.

These three products work seamlessly together to enable companies to implement a comprehensive data strategy, from data storage and management to analysis and planning.

SAP Analytics Cloud is a comprehensive analytics platform!

SAP Analytics Cloud: A platform for all requirements

Our last webinar “SAP Data & Analytics Strategy Update”, held jointly with SAP, focused on the SAP Analytics Cloud and its new functions. This platform has established itself as an indispensable tool for reporting, dashboarding and planning. Here is an overview of the most important new features:

The Data Analyzer offers a user-friendly option for ad-hoc analyses and rapid report generation. Users can gain detailed insights into their data without any major technical effort.

Interactive dashboards allow companies to visualize their data dynamically. Filter options and links enable flexible use of the information and promote collaboration.

The mobile usability of the SAP Analytics Cloud ensures that information is available anytime and anywhere. Whether on a smartphone or tablet, users are always able to act.

Another highlight are the powerful custom widgets, which enable companies to create individual visualizations and functions. With this flexibility, specific requirements can be implemented efficiently

A central component of the strategy presented is the seamless integration of SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere. This combination enables companies to optimize their planning and analysis processes.

Process flow and required steps

Seamless Planning with SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud

SAP is currently driving forward the integration between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere with the introduction of "Seamless Planning". This new function makes it possible to store SAC data models directly in SAP Datasphere, which reduces the data inventory and enables real-time use as well as extended processing and expansion scenarios. The model structure and metadata will continue to be defined in SAC, but modelers can choose SAP Datasphere as the storage location when creating new models or public dimension tables. This promotes Seamless Planning and analysis on a simplified, centralized and managed data platform. All in all, a promising - and long-awaited - integrated solution that perfectly demonstrates the power of BTP.

The process in detail

  1. Establish the connection: SAC and SAP Datasphere are connected to each other.
  2. Create model: New planning models can be created directly in SAP Datasphere.
  3. Use integration: Planning data from SAC can be integrated into SAP Datasphere workflows.
  4. Check access rights: Roles and authorizations are controlled to ensure smooth collaboration.

With this integration, companies benefit from a uniform database that enables consistent planning and analysis. This not only promotes efficiency, but also decision-making quality.

Roadmap: The road to Seamless Planning

The roadmap for Seamless Planning presented here provides a clear outlook for future developments:

  • Q4 2024: Controlled release of first functions.
  • Q1 2025: General availability with extended range of functions.

The general availability of Seamless Planning is planned for the first quarter of release 2025. Prerequisites for use include both the SAC and SAP Datasphere being hosted in the same data center and using the same identity provider. In addition, the respective tenants must be connected to each other in a 1:1 relationship.

With Seamless Planning, companies can accelerate their planning processes, improve collaboration between different business units and make informed decisions based on harmonized and semantically enriched data. Future enhancements aim to further strengthen integration and provide additional functionality to realize the full potential of this powerful combination. Planned enhancements include the persistence of facts and master data and the use of planning data in SAP Datasphere workflows. These steps pave the way for even deeper integration and extended functionality.

Reference architecture based on SAP Datasphere

Data strategy of the future

Another important point is the reference architecture, which integrates SAP Datasphere as a central building block. By connecting source systems such as S/4HANA and ECC with external data sources, this architecture provides a flexible basis for data analysis.

The combination of replication and virtual access ensures that data is available both quickly and securely. This helps companies to implement a scalable and future-proof data strategy.

Rethinking data management

The updates clearly show how SAP is creating the basis for modern data management through continuous innovation. The SAP Analytics Cloud has established itself as a powerful platform that offers great added value through customer-specific adaptations. Seamless Planning and integration with SAP Datasphere ensure that companies can optimize their planning and analysis processes.

The future of SAP Data & Analytics promises further exciting developments and we at PCS beratungscontor remain a reliable partner for tapping into this potential.

entry.title: SAP CIS Identity Provisioning Service integration with SAP HANA Cloud, HANA Database
entry.content:
This blog post is dedicated to the functionality available from Q32024 and which was long awaited by a lot of customers.

The Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) integration, a part of SAP Cloud Identity Services, is now available for SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database (HANA DB) (link to a public HANA Cloud roadmap item and a link IPS “What is new” section).

This integration allows for streamlined automated user provisioning, simplifying the management of user access and roles within the HANA Cloud environment. IPS, as a mechanism for assignments transport, maps assignments coming from user source, such as the Identity Directory, to the one in HANA Cloud DB (like HANA roles). By leveraging this functionality, organizations can enhance their identity management processes directly within their cloud database systems.

In this blog post, we will dwell through detailed guidance on configuring and utilizing this IPS service together with HANA DB.

Prerequisites

IPS features a connector for the HANA DB. This plug-in allows for the management of users and their role assignments. When using the SAP HANA Database as the target system, the Identity Provisioning service will manage all users in the user group(s) where the technical user of the service has the OPERATOR privilege. It is possble to set up the same SAP HANA database as a target multiple times in order to manage different user groups, by defining different technical users for the IPS.

Set up in SAP BTP Cockpit to access IPS as a part of SAP CIS

First of all, you need an access to SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS) tenant, as IPS is a part of it. Above all, please check if you have already assigned CIS tenant, you can do it by following the steps explained here.

If not available, you would be able to get your productive and test tenant as part of a self-service request in SAP BTP cockpit by following the next procedure described in SAP CIS initial set up guide.

Second of all, you would need to configure SAP HANA Cloud “admin-api-access“ service plan (XSUAA instance) to gain access to the Identity Provisioning Service. For that you must have:

  1. SAP BTP global account with a , including the admin-api-access service plan. If you still do not have an account, please see Getting a Global Account and View and Manage Services from the Service Marketplace.
  2. You must have the Subaccount Administrator role collection for the subaccount. Speak to your SAP BTP global account administrator if you do not have this one.
  3. After that, you need to subscribe to the admin-api-access service plan and create a service binding for it. How to do it, we will review in the procedure below.

Procedure

  1. In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to the subaccount where you want to create the subscription to the SAP HANA Cloud multi-environment administration tools.
  2. Create a subscription to the SAP HANA Cloud administration API service plan:
    1. In the left pane of the subaccount, click “Instances and Subscription” and Click “Create”.
    2. Under Service, pick “SAP HANA Cloud”.
    3. Under Plan, pick admin-api-access.
    4. Under “Runtime environment”, pick “other” for multi-environment option. 



    5. Click “Next” to continue and enter the configuration parameters as JSON:

 

{ 
"technicalUser": true, 
}

 

"technicalUser": This is a mandatory parameter which configures whether this client is a technical user. A technical user is a special user for whom all scopes are set as authorities. If the parameter is set to true, the client grants (client credentials, client x509) return an access token that includes all scopes.

6. Click “Create” to finish the creation of the instance.

3. Create a service binding:

1. In your subaccount, navigate to the “Services” -> “Instances and Subscriptions” page.

2. Go to the “Instances” tab and locate your instance. Click the *** (Actions Menu) icon of the admin-api-access service instance you created and select “Create Service Binding” (Multi-environment) or “Create Service Key” (Cloud Foundry) depending on the subscription chosen above.

3. In the “New Binding” wizard, choose a name for your binding and provide configuration parameters either by uploading a JSON file or by entering them in the dialog. For our demo scenario, let’s use the next configuration:

 

{
  "credential-type": "binding-secret",
  "hide-secret": true
}

 

For more information on service binding parameters, see Binding Parameters of SAP Authorization and Trust Management Service.

4. You have subscribed to the admin-api-access service plan and created a new binding for the service instance.

1. Retrieve “the client ID”, “client secret” and “url” through the service binding on the “Instances and Subscriptions “page of your subaccount by clicking on the “View Credentials” button.

2. You will need these values to configure the OAuth2TokenServiceURL, 'User' and 'Password' properties when configuring the 'SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database' system in SAP Identity Provisioning Service. You will also need some of this information to configure your technical user in the SAP HANA database.

Set up in SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (HANADB)

  1. For setup of the HANADB, client-id, certificate and issuer are important that can be obtained using the url retrieved in the above step 4.a. To get the certificate and issuer, you need to visit the  location: {url}/sap/trust/jwt.

Sample response of location: {url}/sap/trust/jwt:

 

{
  "issuer_dn": "OU=JWT, CN=https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token",
  "subject_dn": "OU=JWT, CN=https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token",
  "purpose": "JWT",
  "certificate": "-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----<encoded-certificate>==-----END CERTIFICATE-----",
  "issuer": "https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
}

 

2. To successfully configure JWT authentication trust to your XSUAA client must be established. This process requires the XSUAA certificate to be available. For that, we can use our guided JWT provider creation wizard in Cloud Central. You only need to provide the issuer URL of your XSUAA. The wizard will also make suggestions for all names, so you only need to provide a name for the provider. The detailed guide how to use it you can find in the following 2-parts series blog-post.

Alternatively, the next sql commands can be used from below. They contain some placeholders in angle brackets. These placeholders have to be replaced as follows:

  • identity, issuer, certificate:
    • The values are extracted from the previous step. Please note “identity” value is client-id, “issuer” is the same as issuer and “certificate” is the same as certificate.
  • certificateName, pseName, userName, usergroupName, jwtProviderName:
    • The values are only used internally and can be chosen accordingly
    • Suggested values are (replacing PRODUCT by something that identifies your product) 
      • certificateName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_CERTIFICATE 
      • pseName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_PSE 
      • userName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_USER 
      • usergroupName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_GROUP 
      • jwtProviderName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_PROVIDER 

3. Open HC Central SQL console and connect to the HANA database any user having the CREATE USERGROUP (for user group creation) and TRUST ADMIN (for certificate maintenance) system privileges. 

Issue the following sql commands to create (if not done yet with Guided JWT provider creation wizard): 

  • PSE (certificate collection) along with the trusted certificates 
  • JWT provider for your authentication that uses the PSE to verify the validity of JWTs: 

 

create certificate <certificateName> from '<certificate>' 
create pse <pseName> 
alter pse <pseName> add certificate <certificateName> 
create jwt provider with issuer '' claim 'client_id' as external identity claim 'client_id'='' priority 210 
set pse <pseName> purpose jwt for provider <jwtProviderName>

 

4. At this step, we are going to create a new usergroup for IPS -managed users and technical database user IPS/SCIM to manage the users. We also configure JWT authentication for the user in which we map the client-id value from the admin-api-access to the database user. 

In the example below the user is part of the user group DEFAULT but you can choose to add it to any other user group. The technical user is set as an OPERATOR for the new SCIM user group. This can be done through GUI but for completeness, here are the SQL statements that can be used. 

 

create usergroup <usergroupName> 
create user <userName> with identity '<identity>' for jwt provider <jwtProviderName> set usergroup default 
grant usergroup operator on usergroup <usergroupName> to <userName>

 

5. Finally, the new user created in step-4 is assigned the ROLE ADMIN system privilege to be able to grant/revoke roles. You don‘t need to use ROLE ADMIN but can also use role groups if the IPS/SCIM user should only manage a subset of roles. 

 

grant create schema to <userName> 
grant role admin to <userName> 
GRANT CATALOG READ TO <userName>

 

IPS set up for SAP HANA Cloud, HANA DB

  1. First and foremost, please get yourself familiar with IAM reference architecture as a starting point. As per our reference architecture, the strategy is to have the Identity Directory (also a component of SAP CIS) as a user source (even if you use other solutions as Microsoft Entra ID). 
  2. Then, please go to Identity provisioning app to set up HANA Cloud as your “Source” or/and “Target” systems as per your use case. If you want to do identity provisioning from one HANA (source) to another HANA (target), configuration on both HANAs is needed, as with the following parameters.  

Property Name 

Description & Value 

Authentication 

Enter: BasicAuthentication 

hana.cloud.db.instance.type 

Enter: hdb 

hana.cloud.db.instance.id 

Refers to a unique identifier associated with a specific database instance within the SAP HANA Cloud. 

 

OAuth2TokenServiceURL 

Enter the OAuth 2.0 Token Service URL. 

For example: https://demo-hc-3-test.authentication.sap.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token 

Password 

(Credential) Enter the OAuth Client Secret, created for your SAP HANA Database system. 

ProxyType 

Enter: Internet 

Type 

Enter: HTTP 

URL 

Enter the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database API URL. 

For example: https://api.gateway.orchestration.<cluster>-<datacenter>.hanacloud.ondemand.com 

User 

Enter the OAuth Client Id, created for your SAP HANA Database system. 

Note: Please use the following values for the three parameters as mentioned in the service-binding procedure shown above in step 4.a. (when creating your admin-api-access): 

  1. “OAuth2TokenServiceUrl” as the URL  
  2. “User” as the Clientid 
  3. “Password” as the Clientsecret 

There are few additional optional parameters as shown in the screenshot below that help with debugging. Please refer IPS Documentation for more details on the configuration parameters and transformations. 

3. Trigger the Jobs as per your need: 

Read Job:  

Trigger the read job to provision only the new and updated users from the source to the target. After the read job, new users will be created in target and the state of certain users will be updated depending on the differences. For further information on the read job please refer to the document.  

Resync job:  

This job is specifically used to fix inconsistencies between two systems. It reads full entities from source and updates the entities in target to be in sync with source.  At the end of the sync job both the source and target will have same state in respective to resources - users and Groups(roles) in HANA. Refer the document for further details on sync job. 

The two jobs above are mainly used to manage users. For other jobs please refer to the CIS/IPS documentation: 

  1. Simulate Job (estimates the number of entities that will be created, updated, deleted or skipped)  
  2. Validate Job (verifies the content of the entities) 

4. The output of step-2 would look like following with successful Jobs done: 

With that, you will conclude the provisioning of your users to desired systems.

---

Additional helpful sources:

IAS side: 

What's New for Cloud Identity Services 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Source) 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Target) 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Proxy) 

For Neo environment: https://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database 

the HANA DB beta connector is the one for the HANA DB on prem version.  Among other limitations, this connector is only available for standalone SCI tenants running on the NEO infrastructure. More details you can read on the help page. 

HANA Cloud side: 

Admin guides: 

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/user-provisioning-with-identity-provisioning-service (Setup in SAP BTP Cockpit) 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-administration-guide/user-provisioning (Setup In SAP HANA database)   

Security Guide: 

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-security-guide/user-administration-tools 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-security-guide/sap-identity-provisioning-service

entry.title: SAP Make-to-Order Insights
entry.content:
Today we will learn about the Make to order process used in SAP, the blog will give you an understanding of the business process and the configuration steps.

Strategies which businesses follow to produce goods are as follows:

Make to Order (MTO): The product is not manufactured in advance, Product is produced once the order is received, like Cars, Trucks, Bus’s etc.

Make to Stock (MTS): The product is manufactured in advance, it is produced in mass and fast moving in nature, such as consumer products, pharmaceuticals, household items, etc. 

 

 

Among these two production strategies we will discuss the Make to Order (MTO) business process in this blog.

Make to Order (MTO) Production Strategy:

"Make to order" refers to a production strategy where a company only manufactures a product once a customer places an order, meaning they don't produce goods in advance and stockpile inventory; this approach prioritizes customization and flexibility, allowing for tailored products based on individual customer needs, while minimizing the risk of overproduction and associated inventory costs, but often results in longer lead times for customers as production begins after the order is received.

Example- Cars, Trucks, bus’s etc.

Benefits:

  1. Reduce Inventory cost as production starts after order, saving on storage space of product.
  2. Increase customer satisfaction as they get what they want, Leads to higher satisfaction and loyalty.
  3. Greater flexibility as companies can adapt to changing market demands and quickly introduce new product variations based on customer feedback.
  4. Less waste as no overproduction.

Challenges:

  1. Potential for higher costs as it will be produced for special customers.
  2. Efficient production planning to meet customer deadlines.
  3. Longer lead time as production starts after receiving order. 

When to use?

  1. High production customization, when customer requires unique features or options on their products.
  2. Fluctuating demand: If customer demand is unpredictable and varies significantly, make to order can prevent overstocking. 
  3. High-value products: For expensive items minimizing inventory is crucial. 

Process flow for Make to Order:

Standard Configuration guide to activate MTO:

  1. Item category group: 0001/0002
  2. Item Category: TAK
  3. Schedule line category: CP
  4. MRP Type: ND/PD
  5. Special stock indicator: E
  6. Item category determination: OR + 0001+ Blank + Blank =TAK
  7. Strategy group:020
  8. Requirement Class: 040
  9. Requirement type: KE

Pre-requisites:

  •       In Material master settings that need to be carried out are as follow:
  • In Sales: Sales org. 2 screen enter Item Category group= 0001 (MTO) / 0002 for Configurable material.
  • In MRP1 screen, MRP type= PD/ND (No planning) & MRP controller = 101
  • In MRP3 screen, Strategy Group= 20 (Make to order/No assembly order).
  • In MRP3 screen, Availability check= 01 (Daily requirement).
  • Depending on business requirement you can create or use standard Sales document type, Item category & Schedule line category.

    Process Steps:

    • Create sales order

    Note: Costing for MTO process depends on Sales order, enter EK02 condition manually.

              
    • After creating sales order, we need to check whether the requirement has transferred to MRP using MD04.
    • Requirements planning in (MRP) is a manufacturing process control that includes production planning, scheduling, and stock requirement, enter transaction code: MD02, enter material, plant, MRP control parameters & execute it.
             
    • Convert planned order to Production order using MD04, Choose -> Prod. Order (Convert planned order into production order).
             
    • Release the Production order by clicking on flag and save the Prod. Order.
    • Withdrawing the Material for the Production Order (System creates a reservation for the relevant material components when you make a production order) using MIGO.
    • Confirm the production order using CO11N.
    • Create goods receipt against sales order using MIGO, Enter Movement type as 101.
    • Check the stock overview using MMBE.
    • The schedule lines will be confirmed in sales order, therefore create delivery & post goods issue.
    • Once PGI is done create Billing document.

Expected errors and their prospect solutions are as follow:

  • Error: Sales order does not trigger production order

Prospect solution: Assign correct requirement type to the item category and Ensure requirement class is defined.

  • Error: MRP does not generate planned orders.

Prospect solution: Check strategy group in material master and assign the correct requirement type.

  • Error: Production order does not consume sales order stock.

Prospect solution: Set special stock indicator as” E” in MRP 4 view of material master and ensure MRP settings align with MTO requirements.

  • Error: Sales order confirmation ignores component availability (ATP Not Considering Component Lead Times).

Prospect solution: Check ATP scope in OVZ9 and maintain proper AATP settings and ensure component lead times in material master.

Conclusion: Make-to-Order (MTO) process, stock can be customized, manufacturing can only begin only after receiving a confirmed customer order. Its primary advantages are lower inventory costs, greater customer satisfaction, enhanced production planning, and resource allocation efficiency.

entry.title: SAP CodeJam HANA ML in India 2025-03 Recap
entry.content:
At the beginning of March 2025, as part of the ML/AI Tour my fellow dev advocate @noravonthenen and I did in India, we held five SAP CodeJam events in Kolkata, Pune, and Bengaluru.

Two of these events focused on getting started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python.

Equally important were chances to catch up with some organizers and participants in informal post-work settings...

...or with our SAP colleagues.

I am glad Nora and I could help so many participants to go through the journey of improving their skills working with ML and AI!

Traditionally, I very much enjoyed SAP Stammtisch at the end of the last CodeJam of the Tour.

#IamSAP 

I always take the opportunity to experience at least a little bit of the city I am in, so you can see my photos:

  • Kolkata: https://www.instagram.com/p/DG1wL0OydCp/ 
  • Pune: https://www.instagram.com/p/DG75rf0MPkt/ 

Now, the next stop for me is in Kraków on April 3rd.

Would you like to host such an SAP CodeJam? Here are the details: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-getting-started-with-machine-learning-usin...

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Creating a custom AI agent on the BTP
entry.content:
SAP is going full speed with AI agents on Joule. Indeed, it's incredible how such agents can address complex requests by orchestrating a set of generic tools they are given access to.

If you have a need to build your own custom AI agent on BTP, then this tutorial will hopefully be a good start.

  • Is this tutorial production-ready? No
  • Does it provide inspiration and a head start for your own projects? I would like to think so
  • Can you learn how AI agents work and how to create and extend your own? Absolutely! You will see how to create an AI agent using the open source framework LangGraph on the BTP.

Table of contents

  • Background
  • Architecture 
  • Prerequisites
  • Develop an AI Agent in a Jupyter Notebook
  • Deploy the AI Agent as REST-API
  • Put a sandbox user interface on top

You find all code of this tutorial in this repository.
And thanks go to @CanAbdulla for his early feedback on the blog before it went live.

 

Background

Let's take a common business requirement as task for creating a custom BTP AI agent.

Send an email to Peter and let him know whether invoice XYZ been paid.

In the ideal world you would leverage SAP standard functionality, ie Joule. If that's not possible for whatever reason, we can use BTP to make our own agent. To make it easy to implement this example, the AI agent is not actually connecting to a live ERP system. Instead the response of the invoice status will be faked. However, the AI agent doesn't know that the invoice status is made up and it should not be too tricky to connect the agent to a SAP system.

Instead of hardcoding the workflow of getting the invoice status and sending the email, we provide the AI agent with specific tools (here Python functions) that it can decide on it's own how to use them and in which sequence. These function can take one or more parameters as input and return a response themselves:

  • get_invoice_status(invoice_id)
  • get_email_address(name)
  • send_email(recipient_name, email_address, email_text)

An AI agent shines best when these tools can be used for a number of different purposes. Hence the AI agent is also given these additional tools for you to be creative with:

  • answer_SAP_question(text): Delegates questions about SAP to an FAQ assistant
  • get_text_from_link(link): Retrieves the text from a website. You will see later how this tool is useful in combination with the above FAQ assistant
  • get_lunch_menu(): Gets today's menu from the SAP canteen in Zurich, Switzerland, as example of website scraping. That website is in German only, but you can test whether the AI agent can translate the text to English, or whether this could be an additional tool for the AI agent.
  • get_live_tv_arte(): Obtain a description of what's currently shown on the French/German TV station ARTE, as an example of calling a REST-API. In case you are outside France or Germany you might not be able to see the live stream, but the description of what's on should be visible.

 

Architecture

The architecture of the AI agent can be very lean. At a minimum you just need:

  • SAP Generative AI Hub: To access Claude, the Large Language Model which is powering this AI agent
  • Cloud Foundry: The code of the actual AI agent is deployed as a REST-API on Cloud Foundry. This tutorial also includes a quick sandboxing user interface that is also deployed on Cloud Foundry.

Only in case you want to implement the FAQ assistant that answers questions about SAP, you also need:

  • SAP HANA Cloud: To store the texts and to create, store and compare the text's embeddings
  • Mistral-Large-Instruct: A Large Language Model, that is hosted by SAP for text processing and creation

 

Prerequisites

To follow the implementation hands-on, you require these components:

  • SAP Generative AI Hub with the model "claude-3.5-sonnet" deployed,
  • Python environment, ie Jupyter Notebooks in Miniconda,  for sandboxing and testing
  • Cloud Foundry on BTP if you want to deploy the application as REST-API

Optionally: For the AI assistant to be able to answer questions about SAP, you also need to implement the tutorial Creating an FAQ assistant as tool for a BTP AI agent. 

This blog assumes that you already have some familiarity with Python and Jupyter Notebooks. However, this project could also be a starting point to become familiar with those components. Personally, I like Miniconda to create a local Python environment and local Jupyter Notebooks. The blog "Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud" that was co-written with @YannickSchaper gives an overview of setting up a miniconda environment.

You need to install these packages in your Notebook environment

  • !pip install langchain_core
  • !pip install langgraph
  • !pip install "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]"

Develop an AI Agent in a Jupyter Notebook

All code for the BTP AI agent can be downloaded from this repository. First things first. In the file credentials.json enter your own logon details for SAP AI Core and your own SMTP email server. Now run the code from 010 BTP AI agent.ipynb. Load these credentials and apply them.

import json, os
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"]      = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"]
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]  = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"]       = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_AUTH_URL"]
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"]
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"]       = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_BASE_URL"]   
smtp_server   = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_SERVER"]
smtp_port     = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_PORT"]
smtp_user     = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_USER"]
smtp_password = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_PASSWORD"]

Now we start with the AI agent. The following functions will become tools for the agent. Each function starts with a comment of what the function does and which input parameters it requires. Those comments are useful for us humans, but they are also picked up by the agent to understand what each function does, which input it requires and what it returns. That's already sufficient for the agent to decide whether and  how to use each tool/function.

To make the code work you will just need to make these adjustments:

  • Enter your own email addresses in the get_email_address() function
  • Enter the URL of your FAQ assistant in the answer_SAP_question() function. This is optional, you can also decide not to use this function.

 

Get invoice status
import random
def get_invoice_status(invoice_id: str) -> str:
   """Returns an invoice's status, ie whether it has been paid or not.

   Args:
      invoice_id: The invoice id
   """

   # This function mocks retrieving the invoice status from a live system
   # See SAP's API documentation for the real / live API that can provide this information from your system, ie the InvoiceClearingStatus on
   # https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/19d48293097f4a2589433856b034dfa5/cb3caf09bd6749c59f0765981032b74e.html?locale=en-US
   options = ['Paid', 'Overdue', 'Unpaid, not due yet']
   invoice_status = random.choice(options)
   response = f"The status of invoice {invoice_id} is: {invoice_status}." 
    
   return response

 

Get email address
def get_email_address(name: str) -> str:
   """Returns the person's email address

   Args:
      name: The name of the person whose email address is returned
   """

   # This function mocks retrieving an email address from a live system
   dict = {}
   dict['Ewald'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'
   dict['Stefan'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'
   dict['Fabian'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'

   if name in dict.keys():
      response = dict[name]
   else:
      response = dict['Ewald']
   return response

 

Send email
import smtplib, ssl
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
def send_email(recipient_name: str, email_address: str, email_text: str) -> str:
   """Sends emails. Returns a status update.

   Args:
      recipient_name: The name of the email recipient
      email_address: The recipient's email address
      email_text: The email's text that will be send
   """
    
   # This function uses SMTP credentials to send an email directly from Python
   # For productive use you may want to leverage a BTP component, for instance the Alert Notification service
   # https://help.sap.com/docs/alert-notification/sap-alert-notification-for-sap-btp/email-action-type?locale=en-US
    
   # Prepare email content
   subject = 'Email from your SAP BTP AI Agent'
   content = email_text
   msg = MIMEText(content, 'plain')
   msg['Subject'] =  subject
   msg['From']   = smtp_user 

   # Send the email
   context = ssl.create_default_context()  
   with smtplib.SMTP_SSL(smtp_server, smtp_port, context=context) as server:
      server.login(smtp_user, smtp_password)
      server.sendmail(from_addr=smtp_user, to_addrs=email_address, msg=msg.as_string())

   return f"I sent the email to {recipient_name} ({email_address}): \n{email_text}"

 

Answer SAP question
### TODO Requires the implementation of the FAQ assistant as described in:
### https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/hands-on-tutorial-creating-an-faq-assistant-as-tool-for-a-btp-ai-agent/ba-p/14027300

import requests
def answer_SAP_question(text: str) -> str:
   """Responds to questions about the company SAP

   Args:
      text: The question about SAP
   """

   # Forward the incoming question to the AI assistant's REST-API
   backend_api = "https://ENTERYOURURLFROMTHEAIASSISTANT/"
   user_input = text
   paylod = {'user_request': user_input}
   headers = {'Accept' : 'application/json', 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'}
   r = requests.get(backend_api, json=paylod, headers=headers, verify=False)
   response = r.json()

   # Obtain the response and response log
   faq_response = response['faq_response']
   faq_response_log = response['faq_response_log']

   # Return the response    
   return faq_response

 

Get text from link

To attempt to retrieve the actual answer if the FAQ assistant's answer point to website.

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
def get_text_from_link(link: str) -> str:
   """Returns the text of the given website link

   Args:
      link: The link of the webiste whose text is to be returned
   """
    
   # Header to get automated access to SAP's website content
   headers = {
      'referer': 'https://www.scrapingcourse.com/ecommerce/',
      'accept-language': 'en-US,en;q=0.9',
      'content-type': 'application/json',
      'accept-encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br',
      'sec-ch-device-memory': '8',
      'sec-ch-ua': '"Google Chrome";v="125", "Chromium";v="125", "Not.A/Brand";v="24"',
      'sec-ch-ua-platform': "Windows",
      'sec-ch-ua-platform-version': '"10.0.0"',
      'sec-ch-viewport-width': '792'
   }
   # Obtain the website content
   response = requests.get(link, headers=headers)

   # Simple parsing to get the website's text
   soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, 'html.parser')
   link_text = soup.body.get_text()
   link_text = link_text.replace('\n', '')
   link_text = link_text.replace('Javascript must be enabled for the correct page display', '')

   return link_text

 

Get lunch menu

An example for webscraping in an AI agent tool.

import requests
from datetime import datetime
def get_lunch_menu() -> str:
   """Returns today's menu of the SAP canteen in Zurich
   """
    
   # Scrape the canteen's website
   response = requests.get("https://circle.sv-restaurant.ch/de/menuplan/chreis-14/")
   soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, 'html.parser')
    
   # Get current day of week
   dt = datetime.now()
   weekday_current = dt.weekday()
    
   # If called on weekend, use Monday instead
   if weekday_current < 5:
       weekday_menu = weekday_current
   else:
       weekday_menu = 0
    
   # Get date for which menu will be returned
   dates_raw = soup.find_all(class_='date')
   dates = []
   for day in dates_raw:
       dates.append(day.text)
   date = dates[0] # Past dates are removed from the restaurant page
    
   # Get menus for that date
   menus = []
   menus_raw = dates_raw = soup.find_all(id='menu-plan-tab' + str(weekday_menu))
   menus_all_raw = soup.find(id='menu-plan-tab1')
   menus_all = menus_all_raw.find_all(class_='menu-title')
   for menu in menus_all:
       if menu.text not in ['Lunch auf der Terrasse']:
           menus.append(menu.text)
           
   # Prepare the response with the above information
   menu_flowtext = ''
   for i in range(len(menus)):
       menu_flowtext += " " + str(i+1) + ") " + menus[i]
   menu_flowtext = menu_flowtext.lstrip()    
   response = f"On {weekday_menu}, the {date}, Chreis 14 serves {menu_flowtext}."

   return response

 

Get current program on TV station ARTE

An example for calling a REST-API from an AI agent tool.

import requests
def get_live_tv_arte() -> str:
   """Returns what is currently shown on the TV stastion ARTE
   """
    
   response = requests.get('https://api.arte.tv/api/player/v2/config/de/LIVE')
   data = response.json()
   title = data['data']['attributes']['metadata']['title']
   description = data['data']['attributes']['metadata']['description']

   return title + ': ' + description

 

Now collect the names of these tool functions in a list. This list will make those functions accessible to the AI agent later on. In case you haven't implemented the AI assistant REST-API you need to remove "answer_SAP_question" from the list.

tools = [get_invoice_status, get_email_address, send_email, answer_SAP_question, get_text_from_link, get_lunch_menu, get_live_tv_arte]

 

The AI agent

We are ready for the best part, let's create the AI agent. Get the imports out of the way.

from langchain_core.messages import SystemMessage
from langgraph.graph import START, StateGraph, MessagesState
from langgraph.prebuilt import tools_condition, ToolNode
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_llm
import urllib3
urllib3.disable_warnings()

 

Initialise the Large Language Model and provide it with the list of tools / functions.

llm = init_llm('anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet', max_tokens=300)
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)

 

Configure the AI agent's behaviour with a system message.

sys_msg = SystemMessage(content="You are a helfpul assistant tasked with answering questions about different topics. Your name is 'SAP BTP AI Agent'. Keep your answers short. After giving a response, do not ask for additional requests. Instead of referring to a link on your response call the function get_text_from_link to get the information from a given link yourself. Only use information that is provided to you by the different tools you are given. When sending email include a greeting and a salutation.")

 

Define the Assistant node, which leverages the Large Language model, the bound tools and the system message as basis of the AI agent.

def assistant(state: MessagesState):
   return {"messages": [llm_with_tools.invoke([sys_msg] + state["messages"])]}

 

Build and compile the graph, which defines the AI agent's capabilities. For full flexibility all tools are connected directly to the initial assistant node. Alternatively tools could be specifically assigned and dedicated as subcomponents to other tools.

builder = StateGraph(MessagesState)
builder.add_node("assistant", assistant)
builder.add_node("tools", ToolNode(tools))
builder.add_edge(START, "assistant")
builder.add_conditional_edges(
   "assistant",
   # If the latest message (result) from assistant is a tool call -> tools_condition routes to Tools
   # If the latest message (result) from assistant is not a tool call -> tools_condition routes to END
   tools_condition,
)
builder.add_edge("tools", "assistant")
graph = builder.compile()

 

The Graph is built and can be visualised. There isn't much too see, since there is a Start node, followed by the assistant, to which all tools are connected. And there is an end. After all the code it's just good to have a visual...

 

Now test the AI agent! Start easy with something simple. Has a specific invoice been paid?

user_input = "Has invoice 42 been paid?" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

 

And the answer in this case is:
"Yes, invoice 42 has been paid. The status of the invoice is confirmed as "Paid" according to our records."

How did the agent come to that conclusion? Pull the most important information from the AI agent's log.

messages_extract = []
for msg in agent_outcome['messages']:
    msg_actor = type(msg).__name__
    msg_text = msg.content
    if msg_actor == 'AIMessage':
        if msg_text == '':
           msg_text = msg.tool_calls
    if msg_actor == 'ToolMessage':
        msg_actor = msg_actor + ' (' + msg.name + ')'
    messages_extract.append([msg_actor, msg_text])
print(str(messages_extract).replace('],', '],\n'))

[['HumanMessage', 'Has invoice 42 been paid?'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_invoice_status', 'args': {'invoice_id': '42'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01S8LnoAEMaXo2e7VStSkdA5', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_invoice_status)', 'The status of invoice 42 is: Paid.'],
['AIMessage', 'Yes, invoice 42 has been paid. The status of the invoice is confirmed as "Paid" according to our records.']]

The color coding helps follow the logic.

  • The execution starts the HumanMessage. That's us.
  • The next row starting with AIMessage shows that the AI agent decided to call the get_invoice_status function, passing the invoice_id 42 as parameter.
  • The ToolMessage shows that the get_invoice_function returned the information that the invoice is paid.
  • The last row beginning with AIMessage again shows that the AI agent used that information and phrased a written response.

There was no need for us to program the execution logic. The AI agent was able to work this out on its own, based on the different information that was provided

  • The tools and their descriptions
  • The system message
  • The actual request

Let's make it more complicated and ask to send someone the status of an invoice by email. 

user_input = "Email Andreas the status of invoice 43" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

AI agent: I've sent an email to Andreas with the status of invoice 43. The invoice is currently unpaid but not due yet. The email has been sent to MASKED_EMAIL with the details of the invoice status.

MASKED_EMAIL does indeed mask a real valid email. Just protecting everyone's privacy. And indeed, this email was sent.

What were the detailed steps the AI agent went through for this?

[['HumanMessage', 'Email Andreas the status of invoice 43'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_invoice_status', 'args': {'invoice_id': '43'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_013xPo3kEDLNujfEjnNc3vZ7', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_invoice_status)', 'The status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_email_address', 'args': {'name': 'Andreas'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_013CsFB4ipU4FKtUuJpn9bDo', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_email_address)', 'MASKED_EMAIL'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'send_email', 'args': {'email_address': 'MASKED_EMAIL', 'email_text': "Dear Andreas,\n\nI hope this email finds you well. I wanted to inform you about the status of invoice 43.\n\nThe current status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.\n\nIf you have any questions or need further information, please don't hesitate to ask.\n\nBest regards,\nSAP BTP AI Agent", 'recipient_name': 'Andreas'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_011uCXBEjqCPHpvdX6oj7MWK', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (send_email)', "I sent the email to Andreas (MASKED_EMAIL): \nDear Andreas,\n\nI hope this email finds you well. I wanted to inform you about the status of invoice 43.\n\nThe current status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.\n\nIf you have any questions or need further information, please don't hesitate to ask.\n\nBest regards,\nSAP BTP AI Agent"],
['AIMessage', "I've sent an email to Andreas with the status of invoice 43. The invoice is currently unpaid but not due yet. The email has been sent to MASKED_EMAIL with the details of the invoice status."]]

Here the agent determined the invoice status as before. Then realised that it needs an email address to then send the email. I think that's pretty cool!

Let's test with AI agent with another task. When asking the AI assistant, that is powering the answer_SAP_question() function, for the boss of SAP, the answer will point you to link on SAP's website. Now we want to go further and ask the AI agent the same question. 

user_input = "Who is the boss at SAP?" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

AI Agent: Based on the information from the SAP Executive Board page, the current CEO or boss of SAP is Christian Klein. He holds the position of Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of SAP SE. The Executive Board, which is the governing body of SAP SE, is led by Christian Klein as the CEO.

That's so much better. And it's not a fact that was trained into the Large Language Model. The log shows how the AI agent got the pointer to the website from the AI assistant (get_SAP_answer) and how it's used its skill to obtain text from a website.

[['HumanMessage', 'Who is the boss at SAP?'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'answer_SAP_question', 'args': {'text': 'Who is the current CEO or boss of SAP?'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01QLVPpJRP2UfeqgkL5vBdv8', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (answer_SAP_question)', "To find the current CEO or boss of SAP, you should visit the SAP Executive Board page at https://www.sap.com/investors/en/governance/executive-board.html. This page will provide the most up-to-date information on the company's leadership."],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_text_from_link', 'args': {'link': 'https://www.sap.com/investors/en/governance/executive-board.html'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01K1mJtaZvDwswZK1FWsTTix', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_text_from_link)', "Skip to ContentContact UsService Request Investor RelationsCorporate GovernanceExecutive Board/Corporate GovernanceExecutive Board LeadershipSAP SE Executive BoardThe Executive Board is the governing body of SAP SE. Subject to the requirements of stock corporation law, the Executive Board is committed to SAP's interests and bound by SAP company policy. It provides the Supervisory Board with regular, prompt, and comprehensive reports about all essential issues of business, corporate strategy, and potential risks.Christian KleinChief Executive Officer (CEO)Learn more about Christian Klein\xa0Muhammad AlamSAP Product & EngineeringLearn more about Muhammad Alam\xa0Dominik AsamChief Financial OfficerLearn more about Dominik Asam\xa0Thomas SaueressigCustomer Services & DeliveryLearn more about Thomas Saueressig\xa0Sebastian SteinhaeuserChief Operating OfficerLearn more about Sebastian Steinhaeuser\xa0Gina Vargiu-BreuerChief People Officer and Labor DirectorLearn more about Gina Vargiu-Breuer\xa0Learn more about SAP SE Extended BoardLearn more about SAP SE Supervisory BoardQuick LinksSAP Trust CenterFind a solutionIndustriesFind a partnerTrials and demosFind servicesTrendingSAP SapphireSAP Business SuiteSAP Business Data CloudSAP Business AISustainabilityPartner ecosystemAbout SAPCompany InformationWorldwide DirectoryInvestor RelationsCareersNews and pressEventsCustomer StoriesNewsletterSite InformationPrivacyTerms of UseLegal DisclosureCopyrightTrademarkSitemapCookie statementDo not share/sell my personal informationBack to top"],
['AIMessage', 'Based on the information from the SAP Executive Board page, the current CEO or boss of SAP is Christian Klein. He holds the position of Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of SAP SE. The Executive Board, which is the governing body of SAP SE, is led by Christian Klein as the CEO.']]

 

Deploy the AI Agent as REST-API

Now deploy the AI assistant as REST-API so that it can be integrated into other applications. Here we deploy a REST-API on Cloud Foundry, but you can use any environment of your choice. In case you are new to Cloud Foundry, then Steps 1 and 2 of this Learning Journey get you started. The application that we deploy here consists of 4 files. The full code is available in this repository.  

  1. btpaiagent.py ➔ holds the actual application logic
  2. manifest.yaml ➔ contains for instance the memory requirements and environment variables
  3. requirements.txt ➔ lists the Python packages that will be installed
  4. runtime.txt ➔ specifies the Python version (python-3.12.x)

Before pushing the application to Cloud Foundry you need to make the following adjustments

  1. In btpaiagent.py specify your own SMTP server credentials to send emails. For productive use you may want to store these more securely, ie in the SAP Credential Store. An example is in this blog. 
  2. In manifest.yml enter your credentials for the SAP Generative AI Hub.

Then push your application to Cloud Foundry.

cf push

And you should see the URL of your REST-API!

 

Now an application can call that BTP AI agent. Let's test this from Python with a not so serious request. Let's use the AI agents functionality of getting the lunch menu and the TV program. Should I go for lunch or watch tv?

user_input = "Should I go for lunch or watch tv?"
backend_api = "REPLACEWITHYOURURL"
paylod = {'user_input': user_input}
headers = {'Accept' : 'application/json', 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'}
r = requests.get(backend_api, json=paylod, headers=headers, verify=False)
response = r.json()
btpaiagent_response = response['btpaiagent_response']
btpaiagent_response_log = response['btpaiagent_response_log']
print(btpaiagent_response)

And It's giving a good overview, especially when taking into account that both the lunch menu and the TV program were retrieved in German.

 

Put a sandbox user interface on top

To make all this more usable, here is a simple sandboxing user interface. For a productive project, you may want to use UI5 or SAP Build. This is also a Python application, now using the streamlit package for the user interface, deployed on Cloud Foundry. The full code is available in this repository.

  1. btpassistant.py ➔ holds the actual application logic and user interface
  2. manifest.yaml ➔ contains for instance the memory requirements and environment variables
  3. requirements.txt ➔ lists the Python packages that will be installed
  4. runtime.txt ➔ specifies the Python version (python-3.12.x)

Before pushing the application to Cloud Foundry you need to add the url of your own AI agent in btpassistant.py (the backend_api variable). Then push as usual to Cloud Foundry.

cf push

And this is the fruit of all the work. With a few basic tools the AI agent was able to let Peter know of the status of a specific invoice!

If you have fun with BTP AI agent concept, feel free to post here any tools / functions into the comments.

Or if you want to continue with another example, then @CanAbdulla has a hands-on tutorial for you to create an AI Agent with human-in-the-loop control.

Happy agenting!

entry.title: Team Fight Detection: How Team Liquid Transformed Their Analysis Process
entry.content:
Competitive esports is a field, where speed and precision decide between winning and losing. Analysts and players typically have the daunting task of sifting through hours of game play to get valuable insights that can change the course of a match. Team Liquid, one of the world's most successful esports organizations, is always seeking new methods to achieve that competitive edge. In the Next Level Esports Center on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), Team Liquid can analyze various aspects of all pro-games in an effort to prepare for their next League of Legends match-up and it already helped them to win a number of championships in the past years.

In the past it was not feasible to detect teamfights, since it’s a time-consuming task to analyze hundreds of matches every month. That's why the team required a solution to automate the process of team fight detection in their League of Legends matches.

This article discusses how Team Liquid uses SAP to automate and optimize their team fight detection process, saving time, minimizing costs, and having improved insights into their gameplay.

 

The Challenge: Manual Analysis in a Fast-Paced World

In traditional analysis, identifying and studying team fights — the pivotal moments in a match when two teams clash — is a labor-intensive process. Analysts were required to manually watch every fight, keeping track of vital information, such as which players took part, the duration of the fight, and how the fight impacted the game overall. Because one game can last between 25 and 45 minutes, this process could consume hours of analysts' time, with the outcome being prone to human error.

For a high-performance team like Team Liquid, this inefficiency was a major issue. They needed a more precise and automated system that would quickly detect team fights and provide valuable insights for coaching and strategy.

 

The Solution: Automated Team Fight Detection with SAP

The solution designed for Team Liquid is based on an automated team fight detection system that works very harmoniously with the existing tools on SAP BTP. The system works by analyzing in-game data for specific events, such as damage dealt, player proximity, and the timing of interactions. A range of machine learning models were trained to evaluate their performance with respect to the rule-based model. Using the resulting feature importances together with manual parameter optimization allowed the rule-based system to perform on par with the machine learning models. By defining a series of conditions, the system can automatically identify when a team fight begins and ends — no manual tracking necessary.

The key technology enabler behind this solution is SAP HANA, which provides the computational power needed to process and handle enormous volumes of data in real time. Using the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) of HANA Cloud, the team was able to implement advanced clustering algorithms, which dynamically group players based on how close they are to one another and their participation in a team fight.  The different clustering methods and their parameters were evaluated using PAL's slight silhouette score and Team Liquids’ expert game knowledge. This combination of methods ensures that all the team fights, no matter how complex or dynamic, are captured.

Additionally, the solution utilizes machine learning algorithms to improve the detection process over time, continually increasingits speed and accuracy. By detecting players movements and interactions, the system is able to track team fights across multiple matches, ensuring that analysts can quickly identify and examine critical moments without having to manually comb through dozens of hours of gameplay.

 

Key Benefits for Team Liquid

The impact of this automated team fight detection system has been game-changing for Team Liquid, both in terms of time savings and cost reductions. The team estimates that the new system saves 10,000 working hours annually — time that was previously spent manually identifying team fights, a task which is now completely automated. This time-saving benefit allows analysts to focus on strategically more relevant tasks, such as strategy development and player performance analysis, ultimately enhancing the team’s competitive edge.

From a financial perspective, the system is expected to save $250,000 annually by reducing the time spent for manual analysis. With saved time and cost savings, Team Liquid can shift resources to other resources, such as training players, scouting, or equipment, all of which support their continuous success in the extremely competitive arena of esports.

 

Real-World Impact: Actionable Insights for Coaches and Analysts

While the ability of the system to save time and expense is impressive, the real value lies in the actionable insights it provides to Team Liquid’s coaches and analysts. By allowing the team to automatically detect team fights, the team has better and more stable data available that can be used immediately to improve their decision-making process.

For instance, coaches can now quickly see which players were involved in specific team fights and how those fights impacted the game. They are now able to monitor player performance within such key situations and identify the areas that need to be re-evaluated and improved. With 16.000 analyzed pro-games in the database, this is helping them to recognize patterns in their own and opponents’ behavior to make respective adaptations to their strategy.

 

Conclusion

The team fight detection feature developed for Team Liquid using SAP HANA Cloud represents another milestone in the long-standing co-innovation partnership. By automating the time-consuming process of detecting team fights, Team Liquid has saved thousands of hours, reduced costs, and gained invaluable insights into their gameplay. This solution has empowered the team to focus on what matters most — improving their performance and strategy, both individually and as a team.
